title: Token Optimization & Vocabulary Power
frameworks: Shannon's Source Coding: information has optimal encoding, efficient encoding wins
Token Economics: vague prompt (many rounds, high total tokens) vs precise prompt (one pass, low tokens)
Vocabulary Power Model: named concepts as indexed lookups, vague descriptions as full-table scans
Context Window Budget: total available tokens minus system prompt, minus history, equals response budget
Compression Ratio: tokens spent vs knowledge activated — higher ratio = more efficient prompt
concepts: 'Ward Cunningham technical debt' is an indexed lookup — 4 tokens activate an entire concept cluster
'That shortcut thing where you save time now but pay later' is a full-table scan — 12 tokens, fuzzy activation
Vague prompts scatter activation across low-signal pathways; precise prompts fire dense coherent clusters
TCP/IP works because both sides share vocabulary (SYN, ACK, FIN) — same principle in human-AI communication
Every named concept is a compressed knowledge packet — a key that unlocks a door in the AI's library
At pipeline scale, imprecise vocabulary multiplies into infrastructure cost (tokens cost compute, energy, money)
Context window management: system prompt budget + conversation history + skill injection + response space
Prompt compression strategies: use named frameworks instead of descriptions, reference people by name, invoke patterns
The most efficient encoding wins — Shannon proved this in 1948
context: Token optimization applies Shannon's information theory to LLM prompting. An LLM processes language as tokens — each costing compute, energy, and money. Precise vocabulary produces better results with fewer tokens. Named concepts activate dense knowledge clusters in LLM parameters, while vague language scatters attention across low-signal pathways. This skill enables systematic prompt compression and context window budget management.
